If your main issue is wanting the junitxml results, you can run gabbi with testr or subunit and then turn the subunit output into xml with subunit2junitxml.

```shell
git clone git@github.com:cdent/gabbi.git
cd gabbi
tox -epy27
testr last --subunit |subunit2junitxml  > j.xml
```

_you'll need tox and testrepository install from pypi._

So I'm wondering if I should separate the second commit which adds the threading stuff? But I have to say it is cumbersome to run tests because you only get to know that it failed but not what happened (unless you manually print it out). Thanks for the proposal. It's great to have someone else thinking about this as well. Makes it more like a community effort than a one-man show :)

> I am wondering why system_root and cores_root are distinct values. Shouldn't they be unified as one kind of "FuseSoC Catalogs".

system_root is deprecated since a while back. I thought I had removed the last trace of that already. Oh.. now I see that it's even there in the shipped fusesoc.conf. I'll fix that

> Proposal: Add a fusesoc_catalogs variable to the config (and/or FUSESOC_CATALOGS to the environment), that is a colon-separated list of:
>
> * Filesystem paths, format: /path/to/catalog
> * http path, format https://github.com/olofk/coolip/master/tree/
> * any other provider. I am thinking of a librecores.org API that can be used as a provider, so that librecores can be a catalog.

Adding the possibility to load core libraries (which is what I call the fusesoc_catalogs in your proposal) from a http address is an interesting idea (even though you might want to rethink having : as a separator for web addresses :))

I'd like to extend this a bit however and add a separate section for each library. That would also make it possible to add more features in the future, such as enabling or preventing automatic upgrades, adding dependencies between libraries and so on. It could perhaps look something like this

    [library orpsoc_cores]
    description=Basic core library for FuseSoC
    type=git
    auto-update=true
    
    [library evil_cores]
    description=Collection of cores used for evil things
    type=webdav
    depend=orpsoc_cores

Are you using raw Bluetooth? Raw mode takes control over the card to connect to the controller first before anything else, and since your card is a Wifi+Bluetooth card it kicks your Wifi off as well. You might also run across an issue of raw kicking off all other BT devices too. It's normal, but an unintended side-effect of raw mode.

For more compatibility, try --hidraw. You won't be able to change your LED color (yet) and wireless polling to the controller will be slower, but it's more compatible if things are already connected.

Hmm, in general, doesn't `cuffmerge` do what you're describing?

More specifically, writing to open filehandle currently is the best way to write output. I don't have anything built in that will dump the directives too. They're stored in the `directives` table of the db though and accessible through the `FeatureDB.directives` attribute, so you can just print them to file first.

If your end goal is a GTF file composed of features selected from input databases, then it might be easier and faster to write selected features from each input db to a single GTF file and then use sort or bedtools/pybedtools to operate on the file rather than pay the overhead of database creation.

In v0.5.0 I have introduced the following events to all the widgets:

- `when_clicked`
- `when_left_button_pressed`
- `when_left_button_released`
- `when_right_button_pressed`
- `when_right_button_released`
- `when_key_pressed`
- `when_key_released`
- `when_mouse_enters`
- `when_mouse_leaves`
- `when_mouse_dragged`

They are assigned callbacks functions like this:

```python
def clicked():
    print("clicked")

widget.when_clicked = clicked
```

The function which is called can also accept a parameter and will be passed data about the event which occured.

The event data returned has:

- `widget` - the guizero widget which raised the event
- `tk_event` - the [tkinter event object](http://effbot.org/tkinterbook/tkinter-events-and-bindings.htm)
- `key` - the key which raised the event
- `x` - the mouse's x position relative to the widget when the event occured
- `y` - the mouse's y position relative to the widget when the event occured
- `display_x` - the mouse's x position on the display when the event occured
- `display_y` - the mouse's y position on the display when the event occured

```python
def clicked(event_data):
    print("widget clicked = " + event_data.widget)
    print("mouse position = " + event_data.x + "." + event_data.y)
    
widget.when_clicked = clicked
```

The implementation was pretty complicated, due to many reasons such as:

- guizero widgets already used some tk events and you cant bind 2 callbacks to 1 tk event
- some guizero widgets have more than 1 tk object which needed to bind to a single callback
- some guizero widgets the self.tk object wasnt the object which needed to be bound to events.

... so I have noted in the documentation that this functionality is experimental as it might need to be changed.

That's interesting that they allow that. I don't know if there is an official spec for heredocs, but the wikipedia article mentions "The most common syntax for here documents, originating in Unix shells, is << followed by a delimiting identifier (often EOF or END), followed, starting on the next line, by the text to be quoted, and then closed by the same delimiting identifier on its own line."

If you wanted to get down and dirty yourself, the place you'll want to look is in the lexer, not the parser. Specifically here: https://github.com/virtuald/pyhcl/blob/master/src/hcl/lexer.py#L182-L214

The `t_heredoc_STRING` is what looks for the pattern of a line that has only non-whitespace characters followed by a newline (`t_tabbedheredoc_STRING` is similar but it allows tabs at the beginning). If it finds a line like that, it calls `_end_heredoc` to see if the `value` is the `here_identifier`. If so it does some work to fix up the tabs, fix up the `pos` counter and the `lineno` counter, and then returns. If it doesn't find that it will go back to trying to match a line for `t_heredoc_STRING`.

You'd probably need to change the `t_*heredoc_STRING` functions to check all lines of the heredoc for a line that ends with the identifier and then do all the necessary fixing up to clean up.

I have a work-around for pyfits that seems acceptable, but it's not pretty and probably much slower than if fitsio could do it.  pyfits can't extend random group HDU, but I don't want to create the entire array in memory before creating the FITS file.  So I create a reasonable chunk, write that to a file, close the file, open the file in fitsio (but don't read it), modify the GCOUNT header word, and close the fitsio object.  Under the hood I assume that cfitsio sees the new GCOUNT value and adds zero-filled blocks to accommodate that new size.  The file can then be reopened in pyfits with memmap=True and the new rows can be written to.  It seems to work and be reasonably fast for modest numbers of GCOUNT value increments each time it's appended.

All four sites either issue API requests on the user's behalf if the required credentials are provided (`refresh-token` for DeviantArt and Reddit; `access-token` and `access-token-secret` for Flickr and Tumblr) which requires API key+secret + said user-credentials, or they fall back to "public" API access which only needs an API key and maybe an API secret. OAuth2 calls this "client credentials grant". Tumblr and Flickr, using OAuth1.0a, don't support this directly and let you use just your API key instead.

The API secret (and access token secret) in OAuth1.0 is used to generate an `oauth_signature` value to sign a request and basically tell the API servers that said request has been issued by an authorized client (see [OAuthSession.sign()](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/421a9740a344d6c18358592dfd7970960d87d6c3/gallery_dl/util.py#L491-L499), specifically line 496).

The API key and secret values are read from the config to allow users to specify their own API credentials in case gallery-dl's default values become invalid or something in that regard. Retrieving the API secret is actually fairly pointless, at least for right now, as this would only be useful in conjunction with user-credentials retrieved through `oauth:tumblr` while using a custom API key+secret, which has not been implemented yet.

I managed to solve the problem. 

Namely, the directory where the "libopenvr_api_64.so" shared libraries are located has to be added to the $LD_LIBRARY_PATH. This can be done by typing 
`export LD_LIBRARY_PATH="${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}:/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/openvr"`
or adding it to the bottom of the ~/.bashrc file, if you're using bash.

Secondly, in order to be able to load the library using ctypes it has to be done as cdll.LoadLibrary("libopenvr_api_64.so"), i.e. using the .so extension.

I'll try to make a pull request on github.

Back tot he PR: I think the `get_reactor` API is a problem. Consider plausible code:

```
class MyService:
    def __init__(self, reactor):
        self.reactor = reactor
    def do_stuff(self) # ...
```

If you initialize `MyService` with result of `get_reactor()` in parent process that instance will be broken in forked process. Switching *everything* over to `get_reactor()` is not really much better than `from twisted.internet import reactor` everywhere. The idiom where you pass a parameter in, as above, is *better* since it's more amenable to unit testing with fake reactors.

So I don't want `get_reactor` at all.

I will (very sadly) presume that you need to use Twisted in parent process. Still think it's a mistake, but oh well. So how about this:

1. You do `crochet.setup()`, it starts reactor and everything is normal. It stores PID.
2. You fork to new process.
3. You call `crochet.setup()` again. It notices PID has changed, cleans up old state, creates new reactor object and then changes `__dict__` (or copies attributes) of existing reactor object to that of new reactor object. They should be the same class so that shouldn't be an issue. It then continues setup as normal.

Benefits:
1. You can use reactor before and after fork().
2. No need for destroy so works transparently with `multiprocessing` too.
3. Doesn't break `MyService` idiom above, insofar as doing any of this is not broken.

I can imagine a number of ways to handle that case. My first intuition is to make sure the argument is iterable, but `str`s *are* iterable.

Some ways that stick out to me are:

1. Check specifically for the `topics` argument to be a `str`, log a warning, and proceed with the current behavior (subscribing to a bunch of topics with names of length 1)
2. Check specifically for `str`, put the `topics` argument into a list for the user (`topics = [topics]`), and proceed as normal
3. Raise a `TypeError` if `topics` is not a `list` (disadvantage being that it's conceivable for users to send in a different iterable/collection type)

Option 2 seems like a sane way to handle things to me. Do you agree? Is there a better way to handle the `.subscribe('bare string')` case?

@jdeligt I had a look at this. Short answer: it seems difficult to solve.

The problem is that the `CallData` class is defined dynamically at runtime for each record (so there's actually not just one `CallData`). Pickle expects the class definition for these classes at `vcf.model.CallData` but of course it's not there (it exists only in the local scope of `vcf.model.make_calldata_tuple`).

It might be possible to workaround this by adding these definitions to the global `vcf.model` namespace (adding it to `globals()`), but they would have to be named uniquely (which should also be assigned to `CallData.__name__`), probably based on the current record coordinates or randomly generated. This will get *really* hairy and I don't think we want to go there.

(I'm still not completely sure why my toy example above does work by the way.)

For your specific problem, I would advise to extract the data from the VCF record that you need and only put that on the queue.

Wow, looking into this issue I discovered a true gem in PyYAML that I and apparently some other people don't know about: `allow_unicode=True` option of Emitter.

```
In [2]: yaml.dump({'a': u'asldnsa\nasldpáknsa\n'}, sys.stdout, allow_unicode=True)
{a: 'asldnsa

    asldpáknsa

    '}
```

It's still not perfect, but such unicode stuff was one of the main reasons why I bothered with this bag of hacks, and apparently it's fixable with just this one option (which existed since the dawn of times too!).

-----

Was able to easily fix the multiline-unicode thing with it - that option comes into play in `Emitter.analyze_scalar` method, which sets `special_characters=True` for any unicode stuff without it.

Also added "IMPORTANT" note to the README, as I suspect just that option can probably be used instead of this whole module for most people. Yay!

Thanks again for pointing to this thing, I only wish you came by it waaaay earlier ;)

I think that I can see the problem here, but let's step through it so that you can get an idea of what could be wrong.

Firstly, the error message is fairly clear. At some point  you have a variable of type 'int' where easygui is expecting a string. This is the difference between the integer 7, and the string '7'.

Next, let's look at the easygui call:
```
easygui.textbox('File Path: ' + file_path, 'Total Character Count', (
        sys.stdout.write("%s" % (len("".join(file_content.split()))))))
```

I suspect that this is the line where the exception is coming from. I suspect that if you look a bit more at this then the problem may pop out. 

To do things more methodically, look at the arguments that the textbox class takes. It will make it *much* easier to read and debug your code if you assign one variable per argument and then pass them in. So something more like:
```
msg = 'File Path: ' + file_path
title = 'Total Character Count'
text = sys.stdout.write("%s" % (len("".join(file_content.split())))))
easygui.textbox(msg=msg, title=title, text=text, codebox=False, callback=None)
```

This means that you can debug to find out what some variable (like text, *hint hint*) actually is at the point you make the function call. It may not be a string, as the API requires.

That should be enough to show you where the problem is here, and to help you to diagnose the next one. 

More generally, I'd strongly recommend getting familiar with the debugger as a way of narrowing down the scope of the problem and looking at state before and after it occurs. Picking an IDE like Pycharm can help you out a lot here. Also, if you are interested in doing some reading, then Clean Code by Bob Martin, and Pro Python by Marty Alchin are both excellent (caveats: that's my opinion only, your milage may vary).

Let me know if you still have problems here. Alternatively, feel free to close this thread if you find and fix the problem!

Ah, that's great news. Either GPL2/GPL3 or LGPL are a no-go for me. LGPL is probably fine in practice, but there's scope for lawyers to interpret it incorrectly.

My preference would be for the [MIT licence](http://choosealicense.com/licenses/mit/) as it's about the most permissive of the lot, and has no room for misinterpretation by overly-concerned clients. From what I can see, the only differences between the Mozilla Public Licence and the MIT licence is that the MPL explicitly forbids use of trademarks of contributors by users of the software, and explicitly grants patent rights from contributors to the user (as with the Apache licence).

Lots of licence summaries are available [here](http://choosealicense.com/) if you've not already seen it.

The doc for `statuses/filter` is [here](https://dev.twitter.com/streaming/reference/post/statuses/filter). In there, there's a [link](https://dev.twitter.com/streaming/overview/request-parameters#locations) that explains the `locations` parameter. 

If you read that you will see that those four numbers are longitudinal/latitudinal pairs that describe the bounding box of your location. Twitter interprets the first pair as the southwest corner and the second pair and the northeast corner. Your first pair has a longitude of -71, and your second pair has a longitude of -72. This is not valid because -71 is *east* of -72.  Just switch these numbers and it will work.

```python
exclude_patterns = ['breathe/*']

Addendum: Here's what I see as the key challenges to meet:
- need to be able to differentiate between different types (theorem, lemma, definition, corollary, proposition, example, exercise) for 
    * naming
    * numbering
- need to have flexibility in naming the types similar to the way it is handled in pandoc-eqnos and the others

So after having returned from the walk in the woods with my dog I figured it would be perhaps best, not to differentiate in the `[Term/Name]` bracket part, but rather the `#def:id`? Not sure how the parsing works and what can be included where for the parsing to work and what's predefined, but perhaps this scheme would work:

# pandoc-theoremnos
```
[name]{type:id} 

:    text of theorem
```

## Types (used in `{type:id}` available: 
- theorem
- lemma
- definition
- corollary
- proposition
- example
- exercise

## YAML options for customization:
- `theoremnos-cleveref or just cleveref` - Set to On to assume "+" clever references by default;
- `theoremnos-style-name` with sub-options for each style, that is

For style "theorem" it would be

- `theoremnos_theorem-name: Theorem` - Sets the default name at the beginning of a Theorem
- `theoremnos_definition-name: Definition` - Sets the default name at the beginning of a Definition

- `theoremnos_style-plus-name` - Sets the name of a "+" reference (e.g., change it from "eq." to "equation"); and
- `theoremnos_style-star-name` - Sets the name of a "*" reference (e.g., change it from "Equation" to "Eq.")
with sub-options for each style, that is:

For style "theorem" it would be

- `theoremnos_theorem-plus-name: Theorem` - (e.g., change it from "thm." to "Theorem")
- `theoremnos_theorem-star-name: Thm` - (e.g., change it from "Theorem" to "Thm.").

for style "definition" it would be

- `theoremnos_definition-plus-name: Definition` - (e.g., change it from "Def." to "Definition")
-`theoremnos_defintion-star-name: Def.` - (e.g., change it from "Definition" to "Def.")

## Numbering and naming
- Numbering style and wording set for each style separately, based on `{type:id}`; e.g., `{proposition:PropositionOfSomething}` - this tells Pandoc-Theoremnos that this definition is of the type "Proposition" and defines it as such, using the naming-scheme for this type (i.e., "Proposition" or "Prop." if used with `*@PropositionIdentity` and thus defined in the YAML)
- Naming - whatever is in the bracket [NAME] will be used as name for type of theorem; e.g., `[Pythagorean theorem]{@theorem:Pythagorean}` will return "**Theorem 1 (Pythagorean theorem)**"
- Naming with no individual name - this will result in Pandoc-Theoremnos to use the style-default as name before auto-numbering; e.g., 
```
[Definition]{@definition:DefinitionNature}

:    This is my definition of nature.
```
will result in "**Definition 1** This is my definition of nature"

I'm gonna say no to any of the magical `__call__` or `__getattr__` behavior because it would be a maintenance nightmare. Imagine if people are using some filter we haven't implemented yet and it has weird quirks due to automagically trying to map `.somefilter(somearg='whatever')` and now people rely on that quirk. If we ever go to do a real implementation of that filter we have to maintain compatibility for that quirk, lest we break things for people.

`.filter_` should basically do everything that would be able to be done with `__getattr__` or whatever and make it clear that the user is in a bit of uncharted territory and should use caution and/or submit a feature request to have an official implementation of the filter in question.

Sorry, I assumed that you had client-side code that took over once the page rendered. If you're only pre-rendering, then `RENDER` is useless.

If you're using webpack, you'd probably need to rig webpack's config to export the specific component from the bundle.

Yeah, forever and [nodemon](https://github.com/remy/nodemon) are a good solution for the problem.

Yeah, I generally turn rendering off in development as it's mostly a end-user optimisation that's only relevant in production. It's certainly still worth testing for in dev, but in same sense as testing minified code before a deployment.

There's a setting that you can use to deactivate it: https://github.com/markfinger/python-react#settings

With regards to your question: it's definitely possible, but its a problem that tends to be quite complicated once you start considering the many ways in which people build and/or import code.

If you're using something like webpack to bundle your code into a node-compatible file, you can use something like `fs.watch` or `chokidar` to watch the file and then restart the server.

If you're simply importing the raw files - using something like babel's register-hook for `require` - then it becomes more difficult as you'd probably need to preprocess the files and try to build a dependency graph. It's certainly not impossible, but it'd probably take some wiring to get it working.

If all your code lived in a particular directory tree you might be able to do a recursive watch and then restart the server when anything changes.

Note that you'll probably need to run the server in a parent process so that it can be respawned when it restarts.

Good luck with the investigation.

True, but Django itself doesn't set ATOMIC_REQUESTS for you, even though it could argue that if you don't you are shooting yourself in the foot. Is up to the developer to decide if AUTO_REQUESTS=True is worth it or not (performance, compatibility with legacy code, etc.)
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/settings/#std:setting-DATABASE-ATOMIC_REQUESTS

And I'm comparing it with AUTO_REQUESTS because using the django-reversion middleware is pretty much the same thing. And I'm not sure how many people are aware of the fact that using a third-party library is pretty much setting AUTO_REQUESTS to True.

In fact, if the developer sets AUTO_REQUESTS, wouldn't that achieve what you are trying to achieve with that transaction? Which again I think it should be a decision that the developer of the project should set.

BTW, this wouldn't be an issue if the atomic transaction is only applied the DB operations made in django-reversion.

In summary, wrapping the request inside an atomic transaction when using the middleware is the same as setting AUTO_REQUESTS=True to the project, but this is not done by the developer but by a third party library. Maybe without the user noticing. And if Django doesn't set AUTO_REQUESTS by default, I don't think a library should do the equivalent.

With backports.csv installed in a venv, I see is a consistent warning being emitted that looks like this:

```
(env) $ python -Wall
Python 2.7.12 (v2.7.12:d33e0cf91556, Jun 26 2016, 12:10:39)

So basically any code that happens to `import pkg_resources` will trigger the warning. In my work, it's another pip dependency (raven).

There is an argument that, even from a UX perspective, distinguishing between user name and password validity is not ideal:

http://ux.stackexchange.com/questions/13516/how-to-tell-the-user-his-login-credentials-are-incorrect

Regardless of whether it's possible to get user names on Facebook/Amazon/Gmail, it remains the case that all 3 services do only have a single "invalid username/password" error message. While Kevin Burke makes some interesting points, note that Twilio, where he was at previously, also has a single "The email address or password you entered is incorrect" error on their login page.

I don't think it should be impossible for the user of a package to notify the user of whether it was the username or the password that was incorrect, but I think there's an overwhelming amount of evidence that standard best practice, and hence a reasonable default, is to not be specific here.

The key here is having your ```create_app``` function import the jwt_manager and call ```init_app``` on it, and have another place outside of the ```create_app``` function use the loader methods to register callbacks. # What's happening
See this image, this is produced when I simply add an asset. I provide it a path via explorer, then it automatically sets the value of destination same as file name. 

![bugfix2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13359891/37509495-93b4c54e-291d-11e8-8491-d3750ad928eb.PNG)

As a result, the command generated for pyinstaller is something of the format,
`"D:/mypath/assets/file1.png";"file1.png"`
`"D:/mypath/assets/file2.png";"file2.png"`
The string after the semicolon is for specifying the folder, where the file will live, once it has been bundled by pyinstaller `[--add-data <SRC;DEST or SRC:DEST>] `. 
As a result, if I specify 10 assets, each of them will live in a separate folder.
**As a result, the code fails, because the asset paths get messed up**, from what I was initially using.
As a user simply using your tool, I might not know that this will happen, so I can't specify the expected path in my code beforehand. 

# What I am suggesting
Simply ask the user for the asset file, fetch it, and autofill the `destination` text field with value `asset` no with its filename. No matter how many files the user adds, the default textbox value will be `asset`. This will ensure that all the asset files go to a folder name `asset` in the bundle.
You can ask the user to specify the path to all resources in his code in this format. `asset/file.png`.
Any experienced user will further subfolders in the asset folder will know what to change and what to expect. But for first-timers, this can be overwhelming to debug.  It is cumbersome, but at least it works. In the above approach, the code just crashes.

![bugfix3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13359891/37509508-9e57e27e-291d-11e8-9591-b4accd32734e.PNG)

## Conculsion 
For the destination field, make the default value as `assets` unless user wants to change it.
Experienced user can tailor this folder schema to their needs. New ones, can simply follow.
Direct the user to place his assets in one folder, and use path to that folder in this python script, while using assets. It'll be also nice to tell the user about the [Temp issue and its solution from stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/a/13790741/5371996) when using one file bundled mode with assets.
It'll surely save time for many.

Hope this helps.

aha, pinpointed! @michele-comitini it has to do on how you written the tests... or maybe @gi0baro can shed some light. Fortunately windows is more picky than linux. The fact is that retrieve() returns - correctly - an open handle to the file (so you can fetch its content). 
But! ... unless you call retr_stream.close() before delete()ing the record, the handle is still open and you can't delete the file.

IMHO the behaviour is correct, but lesser-skilled newbies are going to leave open handles everywhere (dunno if this means leakage in linux). 
I very much dislike web2py's behaviour of not ever using "with" anywhere in the code (thing that maybe pushed to write tests without "with" also) but in this case it's the user's responsibility to close the handle before trying to delete something you just retrieve()d

it looks like multiple copies of the server are all starting up at the same time.  That shouldn't be possible as we do some locking/checking that should prevent that sort of thing, but bugs happen.  If ember is concurrently initiating watches for a couple of things at basically the same time then we might see this sort of behavior.

What I'd suggest is shutting down your ember app and also watchman (I believe that something like `taskkill /IM watchman.exe /F` should kill all watchman proccesses) then:

Run `watchman watch-project c:/projects/sc-pp-frontend` to launch the server and start watching your project.

Wait for that to return before you start up your ember app.

Hopefully you'll see things behave more rationally.  If you can confirm that this resolves the issue then we'll re-title it to reflect a startup/locking race condition.

The [easing plugin](http://gsgd.co.uk/sandbox/jquery/easing/), which is still the #1 result on Google for "jquery easing".

The problem we've historically tried to solve with Migrate is that people attempt to upgrade their jQuery core, but don't realize they need to upgrade several other dependencies or change their own code and things mysteriously break. Where possible, and it's usually possible, we both shim the now-broken functionality and give a warning about the fix that needs to be done. The warning should be clear and give the most likely causes. 

So in this case it seems like the most commonly encountered situation by far will be someone using the old jQuery Easing plugin. If it's the first result on Google you can bet people are still using it and it would help them to get a warning. We'll need to punch `.animate()` to look at the function arity and wrap with a new function if it expects the old-style calling sequence, saving `duration` so we can reconstitute the old args at call time?  Or am I over-thinking this?

> Difference between the migrate and core, that in the latter you have to build (if not running in the amd mode which not default) before run the tests, which is not necessary for the migrate, since migrate, by default, would still not use your builded version.

Even then, `npm test` should invoke all tests, including running linters and since both Core & Migrate have additional JSHint setup for the built file, this require building. I'd like to align with Core here.

The `resource` field needs to be set to something useful otherwise you will get all alerts with the same `event` correlating / de-duplicating with each other (because the `resource` is the same `NA` value).

eg. `service=Email`, `event=Availability`, `resource=NA`

The two alerts above will be correlated and you will only see one. This is almost certainly what you don't want.

You can fetch entries matching a specific content type like this:

```python
client.fetch(Entry).where({'content_type': 'content-type-id-goes-here'}).all()
```

You can get the content type of an existing entry like this:

```python
entry.sys['contentType']['sys']['id']
```

The recommended approach for this would be defining your own custom class corresponding to your model, as shown in the main README file:

```python
class Cat(Entry):
    __content_type__ = 'cat'

    name = Field(Text)
    likes = Field(List)  
    # more fields ...
``` 

Then you can simply use:

```python
client.fetch(Cat).all()
```

```
$> git grep -2 abspath git/test/test_index.py 
git/test/test_index.py-        # same file

```

IIRC, at some point I have looked into this issue, and there were no reliable way to obtain un-dereferenced current directory to then create "abspath" without actually dereferencing the path.  Apparently IIRC it is more of a 'shell gimmick' to know that it is e.g. under ~/.tmp instead of physically being under /tmp (as OS would report, and thus abspath and getcwd). First of all, your PATH is fine. :p

Pipenv by default uses *the Python it is installed against* to create the virtualenv, not the first one in your PATH (as you might expect). In your case it is using Homebrew’s `python3` executable (I would expect).

To change the default and make it use your pyenv’s default Python, set the environment variable `PIPENV_PYTHON` to `$HOME/.pyenv/shims/python`.

The previous build system was actually designed so as not to have cython as an install time dependency from the `sdist`.
This is made clear by explicitely including the precompiled `.c` files and `.cpp` file in the `sdist`. The original issue arose because the `cythonize` function in `_build.py` file wasn't checking for  `_harr.cpp` that was generated from `_harr.pyx` it. It was checking for `_harr.c` whch doesn't exist. PR #3311 addresses this issue.

I think that one of two things should be done:
1. `cython` should be removed from the install dependencies.
2. The logic for checking for `cythonized` modules should be removed.

Cython actually [recommends 1.](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/reference/compilation.html#distributing-cython-modules) though I guess that is for us to decide.

@emmanuelle thanks for creating this issue. @mcquin and I are more than willing to help since we’re working on adding volumetric processing and analysis functionality to CellProfiler. You can follow our progress here. We’ll contribute three-dimensional support for some missing common functions (e.g. rank filters).

Most of the problems we’ve run into are usability related.

For example, distinguishing between multi-channel two-dimensional images:

```Python
(row, col, ch)
```

and single-channel three-dimensional images:

```Python
(pln, row, col)
```

requires additional logic when calling certain functions (e.g. skeleton and skeleton_3d). It also impacts CellProfiler logic (e.g. plotting).

While some volumetric operations require a “multichannel” argument to distinguish multi-channel two-dimensional images from single-channel three-dimensional images, it requires further management.

Finally, specifying spacing in volumetric operations can be cumbersome.

I don’t have a solution, it might not be an issue for others, and I certainly don’t want scikit-image’s image container to return. But I hope this feedback is useful for the API stabilization and metadata discussions.

import sys;print("Python: ",sys.version)
import numpy as np;print("Numpy: ",np.__version__)

# Python:  3.6.0 |Anaconda custom (64-bit)| (default, Dec 23 2016, 11:57:41) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)]
# Numpy:  1.12.1
# Numexpr: 2.6.2

a = np.arange(1e6)
b = np.arange(1e6)

%timeit ne.evaluate('sum(where(abs(a - b) > 1e-10, 1, 0))') > 0
# 100 loops, best of 3: 10.3 ms per loop
# 100 loops, best of 3: 7.22 ms per loop (i7)

%timeit np.allclose(a, b)
# 10 loops, best of 3: 61.5 ms per loop
# 10 loops, best of 3: 26 ms per loop (i7)

%timeit np.any(abs(a - b) > 1e-10)
# 10 loops, best of 3: 22.4 ms per loop
# 100 loops, best of 3: 10 ms per loop (i7)
```
Again there is more performance gain in the Numpy version when running on the i7.
Although as said before, most performance was gained by preventing big temporaries.

> I don't understand. Why second node can not will be the quorum if first node was loss? I want autofailover from 2 nodes.

You are mixing two different problems: running etcd cluster and running a postgresql ha cluster with Patroni.
The second one requires the first one.

You can have postgresql HA with only two nodes, Patroni will promote replica to the master if master failed.
But you can't have etcd cluster from two nodes, minimum is three. Quorum can be reached only if majority of nodes agrees on something. Majority of two nodes is two. If you loose one etcd node, you are losing quorum.
https://raft.github.io/ -- explains about raft protocol, which is used by etcd.

> How to restore the cluster DB from the backup (above)?

Are you talking about backup made from existing cluster by wal-e or something else?
Restoring a backup is actually very big and very complicated topic. If you removed an existing cluster (by accident), you can always deploy it once again with the same name and Patroni will restore your data from backup.
But if you are talking about restoring an arbitrary backup into a new cluster, there is another Patroni feature for that: http://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/replica_bootstrap.html#custom-bootstrap

Spilo has support of custom bootstrap, although it is not documented and not really tested, but helm-chart doesn't have it, but again, it should be easy to implement.

I think from reading http://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.4/static/continuous-archiving.html that turning archive_mode on would change the wal level:

When archive_mode is off some SQL commands are optimized to avoid WAL logging, as described in Section 14.4.7. If archiving were turned on during execution of one of these statements, WAL would not contain enough information for archive recovery. (Crash recovery is unaffected.) For this reason, archive_mode can only be changed at server start. However, archive_command can be changed with a configuration file reload. If you wish to temporarily stop archiving, one way to do it is to set archive_command to the empty string (''). This will cause WAL files to accumulate in pg_xlog/ until a working archive_command is re-established.


Although It'd probably be recommended to upgrade to a supported version of Postgres.

> Calculate distance vectors based on PCA transformed data and eigen values

It's technically difficult to transfer `PCA.singular_values_` attribute value from one pipeline step to another. Therefore, you should make class `CalcDist` a subclass of `PCA`:
```Python
class CalcDist(PCA):

  def transform(self, X):
    dist = X * X / self.singular_values_
    return dist
```

Or, if you don't want to subclass `PCA` directly, then keep a PCA object as `pca_` attribute, and interact with it directly in `CalcDist.fit(X)` and `CalcDist.transform(X)` methods.

I think I would probably rather use webpack for more of this type asset management - the django-webpack-loader handles the integration between the webpack bundled packages and Django (with which I am using an .ini file to configure packages that we can read either with Django or Node).

My plan was for all the front end bundle configuration to happen here, so we could define whether the frontend plugin should be registered as loading at page load or as asynchronously loading.

If frontend code is only used in its own templates, it is easy - but we would probably need a way to specify that frontend code should be loaded into particular templates (either in the core app, or in other plugins). This becomes apparent if we consider say, the 'learn' app, where we will have different content viewing plugins (perseus exercises, videos, vector videos, mp3 player, pdf, etc.) that need to be registered as available for asynchronous loading on the learn page.

My intuition is that we could specify in the ini file **app** and **template** parameters (along with sync or async) that would then load those frontend assets for that frontend plugin into the that template, e.g.:

`
[vector-video]
entry_file = js/vector-video-player.js
app = learn
template = learn.html
load = async
app = coachreports
template = preview.html
load = async
`

Or something similar (also happy to shift from ini file use, or different syntax here) this would register the frontend plugin to be loaded asynchronously in the learn.html template of the learn app, and the preview template of the coachreports app (this would probably not happen in practice, as any previewing on the coachreports should be done leveraging the learn app, but I use as an example).

Hello,

The expected behaviour is for the envelopes to be logged when the logging level is DEBUG. 

For example, the suds library that is used by googleads would only log the envelopes of requests if it's logging level is set to DEBUG. The same is true for a popular python library called requests.

I would like to also note that while the default logging level in python is WARNING, both suds and requests libraries would still not log the envelopes of the requests, but only the meta information, for example "INFO: Sending request: GET www.google.com".

One of the reasons why this is an issue is that sometimes you'd just want to see that the requests are happening, but not the requests themselves. It's also not the expected behaviour as I have just shown.

Thanks,

Sorry for the slow answer, I just only noticed your issue.

Recent master do turn-on group update ( reducing the number of UPDATE message generated ) by default. In your example ExaBGP generate an update with two MPREACH attribute ( the place IPv6 routes are stored into ). It would seems that this is what is causing Cisco to be un-happy.

Two things:
 - what I am doing is illegal as not allowed in the RFC ( which is possible and will check )
 - what I do is a grey area of the RFC but some implementations do not support it
 - what I do is valid and this is not implemented by CISCO ( most unlikely case )

The way to fix the solution immediately is to add "group-updates false" to your neighbor configuration while I investigate and fix the issue. Please use the 3.4 branch for production work as master is currently going through lots of changes.

Hi @Resonanz,

I have been using trackpy recently for research and I have also been finding a need to have a GUI like interface to help with particle tracking. Right now, my needs were met by finding an easy way to flip through frames of trackpy identified features or trackpy tracked trajectories. The pims library used in the trackpy tutorial uses scikit-image and I found in there a nice image viewer that can be extended for this purpose. It can be extended with a number of plugins to quite easily create a custom GUI around a simple image viewer similar to ImageJ. See the [ImageViewer api entry](http://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.viewer.html#imageviewer) for more information but the api reference for the [Plugin class](http://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.viewer.plugins.html#plugin) and the docstring on [CollectionViewer](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/viewer/viewers/core.py#L324) really helped me create exactly what I wanted.

To help you out I can post the code I made that allows you to use the image viewer as a replacement for `tp.annotate`. This was done by sub-classing the `CollectionViewer` class and creating a my own `__init__` method and replacing the `update_image` method. Here is the class that I wrote that does the annotation in the ImageViewer:
```python
from skimage.viewer import CollectionViewer
from skimage.draw import circle_perimeter
from matplotlib.patches import Circle
from matplotlib.collections import PatchCollection
import numpy as np
import pims
import glob

class ParticlePositionViewer(CollectionViewer):
    """A subclass of the skimage viewer that will display
    the position of particles on the images in a pims 
    object.

    Parameters
    ----------
    image_collection : ND image collection
        
    df_positions : DataFrame
        needs to contain columns ['frame', 'x',
        'y'].
    """
    def __init__(self, image_collection, df_positions, pos_columns=['x','y']):
        CollectionViewer.__init__(self, image_collection)
        self.df_positions = df_positions
        self.artists = None
        self.circle_kwargs = dict(edgecolor='r', radius=6, facecolor=None, fill=False, lw=1)
        
        self.pos_columns = pos_columns

        image = self.image_collection[0]
        
        # Use the min/max of the first image for all other images
        self.vmin_vmax = (np.min(image), np.max(image))
        
        self.update_image(image)
        
        
        
    def update_image(self, image):
        """Update displayed image.
        This method was overwritten in the skimage viewer. This method will
        plot the particle positions on the image.
        """
        if self.artists != None:
            self.artists.remove()
        image_df = self.df_positions[self.df_positions['frame']==self.index]

        # Add the circles over each particle
        patches = [Circle((v[self.pos_columns[0]], v[self.pos_columns[1]]), **self.circle_kwargs) for idx,v in image_df.iterrows()]
        collection = PatchCollection(patches, color='r', facecolor='none')
        self.artists = self.ax.add_collection(collection)
        
        
        self._update_original_image(image)
        self._image_plot.set_clim(self.vmin_vmax)
```

If you have a `DataFrame` of identified features from trackpy and you already have a `pims.ImageSequence` object, you can call the image viewer using a function similar to `tp.annotate` like the one below:
```python
def annotate(df_positions, pims_obj):
    """Function to display the localized positon of particles in
    an image viewer.
    """
    viewer = ParticlePositionViewer(pims_obj, df_positions)
    viewer.show()
```

I have also made a sub-classed `CollectionViewer` to make a way to flip through my frames and show the trajectories of my particles using color tails to show the last, e.g. 3, frames of the particle's location on each frame. Let me know if you are interested in that as well.

I am not saying that an entire GUI for trackpy should be written by sub-classing scikit-image, but the `CollectionViewer` provides a very good base for handling an image stack and can be extended. It may even be used as part of a GUI where a tkinter window controls parameters.

However, to answer your original question about embedding matplotlib in tkinter you can look at an example in the [matplotlib documentation](http://matplotlib.org/examples/user_interfaces/embedding_in_tk.html) for an example.

Coincidentally, I have been meaning to talk to @nkeim (and maybe also @caspervdw who responded with a similar answer while I was typing this one) about adding some of my code to the repository. The way I have things written may not work well for everyone as for loops are used to draw objects which would probably get quite slow for users with lots of particles.

This is how conda works.  It's not something that conda-build controls.  One of conda's optimization parameters is minimization of the number of features.  If there is a way to achieve an environment that means dropping the feature, conda will do so.  If you list both mesalib and cdms2, then conda has no choice to drop the feature.

Arguably, it's a bug that conda chooses to drop a feature that is installed by some earlier step.  I think conda 4.4 will address this, as it keeps track of things that were manually installed vs. installed as dependencies.

> user goes to install python: conda install python (a very basic operation that will pull down python and 4 supporting files - see details below)

This is by no means a "very basic operation". After all, when the user types this command, `conda` needs the following information:

- Which versions of python are available?
- What are this packages' dependencies?
- What versions of those packages are available?
- Do those packages have any second-level dependencies?
- If so, which versions of those packages are available?
- etc. etc.

It also needs to incorporate information about the target environment. It is possible, for instance, that some of the versions of Python or its dependencies are incompatible with the current environment, and suitable substitutes must be sought.

I think a full understanding of the task `conda` is required to perform would quickly lead you to agree that the download of the `repodata.json.bz2` files is in fact a rather *efficient* way to communicate the information it needs. 

Again, there's no doubt that improving conda's _caching_ of this repodata would provide a significant benefit. But we simply can't do _without_ that information.

one other thing that is maybe worth consideration: keeping a `base-image` branch that installs heavyweight dependencies and creates an image to serve as the `FROM` target for the Dockerfile in master. Assuming you stick the resulting image in ECR or another repository, this has a couple of advantages:

- building new images when developing becomes incredibly fast
- the ubuntu PPA's habit of nuking GDAL packages without warning becomes less of an ongoing headache -- the base image will still be around even when the PPA has betrayed you, making upgrading packages a `when-it's-convenient` rather than a complete blocker.

The images in your code are floats between 0 and 255. Matplotlib's `imshow` expects either integers between 0 and 255 or floats between 0 and 1.

Just divide by 255, then you see that the adversarial is actually very close to the original ;-)

```
    plt.subplot(1, 3, 1)
    plt.imshow(image / 255)

    plt.subplot(1, 3, 2)
    plt.imshow(adversarial / 255)

    plt.subplot(1, 3, 3)
    plt.imshow(scipy.linalg.norm(adversarial - image, axis=-1))
```

> make it so that it doesn't check your local time zone abbreviations when parsing

This part I see the appeal of; the fact that you and I get different parse results based on our local timezones definitely makes debugging harder.

But I don't see why this would exacerbate the problem.  For one thing, I'm suggesting an opt-in, maybe something like passing `tzinfos="US"`.  For another, it specifically means that EST, PST,... will be parsed without depending on what timezone the user is in.

Also keep in mind that if/when #540 goes in, a lot of previously-silent bugs will start raising.  I _strongly_ suspect that `parse("2017-12-08 12:05 EST")` is a common case on which we currently incorrectly+silently return a naive datetime for non-EST users.

While I think the `"Why does EST work but PST doesn't?!?!"` concern is misplaced, I can imagine a variant `"Why does CEDT work but Transylvanian Obscure Time doesn't!?!"`.  That's a shortcoming that I'd be OK with, but can understand the reasoning against.

@nimrand @Qwlouse in retrospect, yes I think that the config scopes are to me the most magical part of sacred. I was thrown off in the beginning by experiments being objects, but I've grown to appreciate that design choice. I think it makes sense for the current approach of defining an experiment in a single module. 

However, my intuition for how to use the experiment object breaks down for larger projects where one would want to keep different components in separate modules. It's this use case that I think an OO interface would be useful for.  

In the context of @Qwlouse being unhappy about the design of ingredients, I wonder if it would make sense to use the current interface as a 'light' interface to sacred without support for ingredients, and use the OO interface as the full interface, including ingredients.  My thoughts being that ingredients seem to map naturally onto a OO structure.  

Aside: recently I've been looking into options for building reproducible data pipelines. Sacred has enabled me to keep the model half of my projects on lockdown, but the data half is still the wild west. [pachyderm](https://www.pachyderm.io/)  is the closest thing I've found to what I'm looking for, but seems rather more complicated than is warranted. All I want is a linear pipeline that uses a hash list of configs to enforce reproducibility and lazily builds data.[ Here's a draft of the base class skeleton](https://gist.github.com/rueberger/bd7b504a7afbfa935e51f75829d789d9). I mention this because it's right up sacred's alley, and if ingredients are getting redesigned anyway... This should probably be its own issue though.

@Qwlouse, that looks good to me. If namespace pollution is an issue, why not store it under `self.config` or something? Similar to your 1st suggestion in this thread. I wonder if named configs are redundant with an OO interface - could be accomplished by subclassing the primary experiment object and overriding `__init__`.  

Pragmatically, @Qwlouse, would you mind giving a rough overview of what the required changes would be?

You can tell this function is a player here because it adds extra parameters to some relations. You can see this in the derivation: `INV` is getting 11 arguments, though you defined it to take 9.

When I saw the issue, I had some data getting passed untouched through a recursive relation: `x` in `A(x,y) <- y'=f(y) && A(x,y')`. I got as a satisfying counterexample a chain that looked like:
```A(x,y) <- y'=f(y) && A(x,y')

Some other suggestions:

1. Avoid alloc_svect in higher-level code. Use instead the svector or buffer classes. This also solves memory leaks down the road.
2. Avoid std::map and in particular don't use this for heap-allocated pointers because behavior will not reproduce between calls. Use obj_map, which uses the identifiers of objects, such as expressions for indexing. Generally, Z3 does not use std::map and instead uses the custom hash tables. This also ensures that all memory allocations use the shared allocator and that memory pressure, allocation counts get detected as a realistic measure.
3. Use the ast_manager::mk_fresh_func_decl/mk_fresh_... routines when creating fresh function symbols/constants. You don't have to maintain your own name space or counters then.
4. Use expr_ref and expr_ref_vector when accumulating intermediary expresssions. Don't use alloc_svect to allocate a raw array and populate it with expression pointers. ast/ast_util.h contains a few utilities for taking expr_ref_vectors into conjunctions and disjunctions. More shorthands can be added by request.

> The interface seems good as well, although I guess we should support just shuffle=True for users who don't care. We could still have shuffle accept a RNG to minimize the number of keyword arguments. 

Done. `shuffle` now accepts `None`/`False` for in-order, or to shuffle, pass a `RandomState` or `True` to use Lasagne's default RNG.

> And instead of circular I'd have a slight preference for forever or epochs=-1 (where the latter would allow specifying the number of repetitions you want). circular sounds like it would repeat the same permutation over and over.

Done. `circular` now replaced with `epochs` that specifies the number of repetitions, or `-1` for infinite.

> And finally, for some purposes it'd be good to return the remainder batch (the last len(datapoints) % batchsize items ) instead of dropping it, there should be an option for that.

The remainder batch is always returned; I have updated the `ArrayDataSource` to make this more obvious. Should there be an option to discard it?

> I'm a bit puzzled about CompositeDataSource. It makes sense to have it so that you can combine multiple callables or arrays, but I'm not sure about the shuffling behaviour pulling different items from the different sources, instead of the same items. If you want that, itertools.izip on the generators would be the more obvious option.

`CompositeDataSource` does use `itertools.izip` underneath. The main benefit is that the `batch_map` and `mean_batch_map` methods are inherited from `AbstractDataSource` making it nice and easy to iterate-map-reduce :) . You could use `itertools.izip` and the `batch_map` function to achieve the same effect, so `CompositeDataSource` is mainly syntactic/API sugar. Would some improved documentation and examples help?

>  (And in your case, where both sources are the same, you could just double the batchsize and feed the two halves to the two training functions.)

Yes... If both sources were repeated infinitely. If you wanted a complete epoch for each, but with different permutations, and you cared enough that each side should see each example once and only once then permuting separately would be desirable.

> In short, I would expect CompositeDataSource(ArrayDataSource([x]), ArrayDataSource([y])) to be equivalent to ArrayDataSource([x, y]).

Hmmm... I see where you are coming from. Would there be a way of structuring the API or documenting the system to explain this better? I guess my intention is that an `ArrayDataSource` will iterate through the elements 'in sync'. I also want to provide a way of iterating over different sections 'out of sync' (this has to happen when different parts of the data set have different lengths, such as in semi-supervised learning scenarios).

> with_params: Hmm, this raises some additional questions. What if you pass circular=False to the outer batch_iterator call? Will this overwrite what you gave to with_params? I find it a dangerous path to mix the dataset representations (DataSource) with the iterators over them

`with_params` overrides keyword arguments. As for the risks, I can see where you are coming from. Another option would be to pass settings such as `epochs=-1` to the constructor of `ArrayDataSource` and store them as attributes. Thinking about it, this looks like a good option.

>> I think it might be a good idea to replace this with a sample_limit parameter

> Hmm, why?

Some times I have to change my batch size due to memory constraints etc. It may be more natural to specify such limits in terms of number of samples rather than number of batches, which could depend on batch size.

> > An additional class to add data augmentation into the pipeline. I think that this would make sense.

> I'm unsure how much of this belongs into Lasagne. I had long been planning to tidy up my data iteration module and release it (as a separate project). It would be good to have something in Lasagne to reduce the boilerplate needed to run an experiment, because not every user plans to write their own framework, but it cannot be as full-fledged as a library dedicated to data iteration. It should just provide the means to either train from simple array-based sources or plug in whatever more complex data iteration code you have.

I was thinking of something very simple, along the lines of:

```py
ads = ArrayDataSource([X, y])
augmented = ads.map(users_augmentation_function)
```

The user would still have to implement the augmentation themselves; we could provide them with a simple hook. The documentation could provide a basic data augmentation example. More something to inform the user 'this is an easy way to add data augmentation into your pipeline' rather than to provide them with a complete implementation.

So another way of orchestrating this is:
just have a `state_history` list, that contains a list of states, and a `current_position` int that indicates where in history you are. The way I've done this in other settings, a `current_location` of `0` means you are at the most recent state.
Undo increments `current_position`, and redo decrements `current_position`.

Therefore, when accessing the current state:
`current_state = state_history[state_history.length - current_position]`.

Actions that result in a new state do `state_history.slice(state_history.length - current_position).concat([new_state])`.

* `baseUrl` - our current jupyter extension base _`URI`_ (e.g. `/user/fvzhyd`)
* `serverUrl` - what we've set up for host records
* `serverConfig.endpoint || serverConfig.url` same thing in rx-jupyter, which is used as the full base URL `https://hub.example.org/user/fvzhyd` yet _can_ also function as the pure base path on a localhost server `/user/fvzhyd` but not in all cases (notably websockets)

So this is pretty weird, since I've been using pyvex on windows for the last two months without issue.

For starters, we expect you to already have the dev tools loaded in your environment, e.g. by using the visual studio developer command prompt. Calling vcvarsall directly seems like a huge can of worms, considering that a) multiple versions of it can be installed on a given system, b) makes it a serious struggle to do cross compiling (something that I have to do for distributions), and c) requires a registry lookup and complicated logic to handle registry lookups and versioning (e.g. visual studio 2017 has changed vcvarsall to VsDevEnv) which honestly I don't think belongs in the codebase.

As far as I'm aware, libVEX is a c99 + gnu extensions codebase. We hacked around the gnu part with preprocessor macros, but you do need visual studio 2015/2017 in order to compile it.

Finally, I'm not sure what you're talking about with the .obj issue. If you look at the bottom of [the makefile](https://github.com/angr/vex/blob/master/Makefile-msvc) you'll see that we compile each object individually, setting the output to a .o file.

Hi again,

OK the job submission broadly has the following constraints:

Ganga doesn't know what (if anything) has been changed in a project.
i.e. Ganga has no way of querying does this LHCb application need to be rebuilt each time.
How should Ganga construct the environment for the job on the worker node?
i.e. How if something has changed should this overload the default components on the worker node.

These 2 problems are solved by the new LHCb build system having a Ganga build target.
This builds all of the required files needed to run a job into 1 `.tar.gz` target file.

This allows Ganga to capture this build-target for an application, arrange for it to be transferred to the worker node, then it additionally provides a consistent entry point which handles all of the environment initialisation within the LHCb build system.
This has the additional advantage that running on the scripts out of the Ganga build target is executing the same commands as you would when running locally, so less differences between Local/Batch/Grid running.


In the case that a user just wants to run a vanilla release of DaVinci with different options to production this is a special case.
Ganga (GaudiExec) still requires for the Ganga build target to exist for the vanilla build target. This makes life difficult as either there has to be a pre-built target distributed by CVMFS which Ganga can use or Ganga has to wrap up the default application build-area you get from an `lb-dev` command.
Distributing a pre-built target on CVMFS unfortunately has several disadvantages which includes a lot of code to look for a specific build-target, and, if there is a bug in the release would require some horrible hacks to work around it. (you can't (well really, should NEVER) change a file on CVMFS)
It was decided after some discussion it is much easier to request users to build a simple target once for submitting default DaVinci applications as this allows for fixes from the LHCb lb-scripts to be rolled out into production rather than requiring a re-release of all of the LHCb apps effected.
The situation may have changed, and if so it might be sensible to re-visit but I think the original ideas pushing for this model of working are better.

There is a question about whether the LHCb documentation should teach people how to use the tools or how they work and I'm strongly leaning toward the documentation being re-written with more of a focus on the latter.

as @elifriedman pointed out, DQN is build to handle discrete action spaces with finite (and not too large) number of actions. See discussion here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7098625/generalizing-q-learning-to-work-with-a-continuous-action-space?utm_medium=organic&utm_source=google_rich_qa&utm_campaign=google_rich_qa
The gist of the problem is the following - action selection in Q-learning involves evaluating Q function over all possible actions and choosing the action with largest Q value, which is not possible for continuous action spaces. This problem can be tackled by using policy gradient methods in which the policy outputa the action directly (see e.g. this paper https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.02971.pdf). 
In terms of practical advice - baselines offer multiple policy gradient methods that can be applied to continuous action spaces (A2C, PPO, TRPO). For an example of using PPO with continuous action spaces, see here: https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/ppo2/run_mujoco.py

OpenThread's CoAP implementation currently combines Client and Server functionality on a single UDP socket.  The TMF Proxy on the NCP utilizes the same CoAP instance on the NCP.  To utilize separate UDP source ports between TMF Client interactions originated by the NCP itself and those provided via the TMF Proxy, we could either (i) create a second CoAP instance on the NCP to support the TMF Proxy or (ii) push the CoAP Client logic into the Border Agent on the Linux side and have the TMF Proxy sit directly on top of a UDP socket on the NCP (rather than the CoAP instance).

Controlled vocabularies and data model are used in Pmag GUI and MagIC GUI, so I'm guessing you mean that you don't need them in Demag/Thellier GUIs.  

In that case, if you are running Demag/Thellier GUI by themselves, or running conversion scripts on the command line, those would be the times when you shouldn't need vocabularies/data model, correct?  

We would then also need to add a no data-model flag for MagicDataFrames, which also grabs the data model by default.  Contribution.remove_non_magic_cols would break, along with some of the ways of instantiating MagicDataFrames -- currently you can create MagicDataFrames with default required columns or building up the column names by providing column group names.  This wouldn't necessarily be a big deal if you aren't using any of those options, but I'm a little afraid of this sneakily breaking things down the road when a Contribution or MagicDataFrame object is missing these things.  

I think I have prevented all (or almost all) redundant calls to Earthref, which means that the slowness should occur only in the beginning.  However, it can be significant depending on your internet connection, which is definitely annoying.  

As I think it through, I lean a little more towards the caching option, honestly.  Another unrelated argument for caching exclusively is to prevent issues like the one you caught earlier today #140.  Caching means we would still be reading in data_model/vocabularies at times when we don't strictly need them, but it's pretty fast to do so.

> 1) When i run the tox command in the venv, i get an error saying "InterpretorNotFound: python" for versions 2.6, 3.3 and 3.4. Do i need to install them into the venv explicitly ?

No. `tox` will use the versions of Python you have available (in your case Python 2.7 and Python 3.5) and error out for any missing versions. Most Certbot devs don't worry about these errors as the tests will be run in Travis for all Python versions. You can get a prettier error message here by running `tox --skip-missing-interpreters`.

> 2) For #3337, do i have to do anything else apart from adding the the "certbot_compatibility_test.sh" in the tox.ini file ?

Do you mean `modification-check.sh`? To resolve this issue, you should add a new `testenv` to `tox.ini` (see the current file for examples) that simply runs `modification-check.sh`. See [this](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/tox.ini#L101) for an example. You should add the `testenv` to the `envlist` in the top of the file so it's automatically run when the user types `tox`.

We could do things like have `tools/venv.sh` use the `certbot-auto` requirements file and then `pip install` certbot in editable mode for example. I'd be open to other ideas too, but I'm not convinced this is beneficial for any packaging efforts and we can accomplish this for devs without affecting packaging.

A self signed cert will not help in any way with the scenario you describe.

Since self signed certs are by definition not valid anyone can replace the node (e.g. via DNS spoofing, ARP poisoning and probably ten different other ways) with a compromised one that equally serves a self signed cert.

The only thing self singed certs improve over not using TLS is transport encryption, but for our use case that doesn't matter since all the data is public on the blockchain anyway.

Though... on further thought... maybe it is reasonable to ask the interpreter to keep a record of all unawaited coroutines?

The implementation I'm imagining is: each coroutine object has 2 extra pointer values embedded in it. In py36, the minimal size of a coroutine is at least:
```python
>>> async def f():
...     pass
... 
>>> sys.getsizeof(coro) + sys.getsizeof(coro.cr_frame) + sys.getsizeof(coro.cr_frame.f_locals)
736
```
so adding 2 pointers would only increase the size by ~2% at most. We use the two pointers to maintain a doubly-linked list, protected by the GIL: there's a global/thread-local holding the list head, and when a coroutine is created, it inserts itself into the list, and when it's awaited, it removes itself from the list. For an intrusive doubly-linked list these are both O(1) and very cheap, just a few pointer assignments: e.g. to remove you do something like `self->prev->next = self->next; self->next->prev = self->prev; self->next = self->prev = NULL`. And then there'd be some object in `sys` or somewhere that let one examine and clear the list.

The list references could be either strong or weak. Implementation-wise, strong refs would mean adding some `Py_INCREF` and `Py_DECREF` calls when adding/removing from the list. For weak refs, we'd leave out the `Py_{INC,DEC}REF` and make `tp_del` unlink. (Note that while these refs would have weak-ref semantics, because they'd be built-in to the coroutine object there wouldn't be any reason to involve the official weakref machinery and `__weakrefs__` and all that.)

Semantics-wise, the difference would be: say we have a piece of code like trio or pytest-asyncio that wants to start tracking unawaited coroutines. With strong refs, the tracking would have to be disabled by default (to prevent memory leaks), so our code would have to be careful about turning it on and off again at the right places, but in exchange it would get exact and reliable tracking of unwaited coroutines. With weak refs, the tracking could be on by default, but we'd also need to hook the warnings module to get full tracking, because sometimes a coroutine might get allocated and then GC'ed in between checks.

The advantage of this is that after things go wrong we get full access to the coroutine objects to decide what to do with them – in particular we can get the coroutine name, but can also filter based on attributes, ask tracemalloc if it knows where the object was allocated, etc. Yet, *until* things go wrong, it's still very cheap -- on the same order of magnitude as a simple counter.

Hey, sorry for being slow to get to this. Even though you can't see it here, I've been thinking about it (and the annoying problem of how to best wrap that IO class hierarchy) all week...

# High level comments

I'm not a huge fan of the metaclass thing, but there aren't any really *nice* solutions here, and it's local and well-contained so meh, we can switch it out for something else later if we want. (I suspect in the long run we may want to define some delegation helpers that work as class decorators and inject methods at class definition time. But there's no reason to try and solve that design problem here; async file IO already raises enough tricky design problems :-)

Regarding the overall public API: So far the rule has been that if there's a standard library module `X` and a module `trio.X`, then the trio version's public API is a mirror of the stdlib version. That's not the case here. Nor should it be: it doesn't make sense for trio to be re-exporting all the stuff in `io`. (Also `trio.io` would be a super confusing name given that like, everything in trio is about io in one way or another. I don't know why Python's designers thought that "io" was a good short name for "file io". But this is just me being grumpy.) So, my suggestion is that we move the stuff in your `io.py` and `io/types.py` to a `_file_io.py` file, and have it define `open_file`, `wrap_file`, and `Path` objects to re-export from the top-level `trio` namespace.

I have mixed feelings about whether to make the `AsyncIO*` classes public, except via `wrap_file`; it's just asking for someone to subclass them or something. We definitely shouldn't make `AsyncIOBase` public, because as a concrete class it's useless – it doesn't even have `read` or `write`! I guess for the other ones I'll probably come around eventually...

Regarding the "`async with await open(...)` nonsense": I agree that it's nonsense, but it's nonsense that accurately reflects the standard Python semantics, so I actually think we should keep it that way :-). And the trio style is quite strict about enforcing the idea that async functions are just functions that you call with `await` – I don't want to have functions that support both `await foo(...)` and `async with foo(..)` as calling conventions. Obviously the way coroutines work under the hood makes it *possible* to get up to all sorts of shenanigans with them, but that's not unusual... generally in Python you *can* do *anything*, but most of it's in bad taste, so we don't :-).

Also, it should be totally legal to write code like:
```python
file_obj = await open(...)
async with file_obj:
    ...
```
so we need `__aenter__` and `__aexit__` methods on `AsyncIOBase` in any case.

Regarding custom handling for `Path.open`: I think this is no big deal? We can use the generated wrappers for the easy methods, and for the methods that need special handling we can write the wrapper methods manually. There's no rule that you can't use both techniques within a single class definition :-)

Speaking of which, `AsyncIOBase.close` also need to be written manually, because `close` methods have special semantics: they guarantee that even if they're cancelled, they still close the object before returning. This is a bit awkward given the way we're building on top of `run_in_worker_thread`, so I think the best solution is to write it like:
```python
    async def close(self):
        # run_in_worker_thread will check for cancellation; make sure it can't be cancelled
        with _core.open_cancel_scope(shield=True):
            await run_in_worker_thread(self._wrapped.close)
        # but if there was a cancellation, we *should* raise `Cancelled`, now that the file is safely closed
        await _core.yield_if_cancelled()
```
As an optimization it might also make sense to check `self._wrapped.closed` before spawning a thread to call `close`. This whole async `close` thing is also pretty awkward given that e.g. socket objects in trio have *synchronous* `close` methods, but I don't know that there's much to be done about it. I guess we could call the file `close` methods `aclose`, like async generators have.

Regarding the threadpool thing: not sure what you're thinking of. Probably just some issue where I was mumbling incomprehensibly to myself :-). `run_in_worker_thread` is the right thing to use here.

The reason i suspect that `__annotation__` is needed is that when I specify a function with argument specifications then `dir` the function that is the only information I can find that holds the info.  But I may be mistaken.  I am not a particularly skilled python programmer most of my experience is C++ and Java.

As to the load hook, it isn't all that complex, but I haven't seen much docs on the process.  

There appear to be two paths for loading classes in the jpype code.  
1) Classes created explicitly from python.
2) Classes created implicitly as the result of a return.

The process is as follows.
1) There is a call to C++ to create a JPClass instance as a result of cache miss in the TypeManager.
2) The class preloads as much as stuff as it can including all the methods.  See `native/common`
3) But it does not actually process those methods yet because processing the methods arguments may trigger more class loads so those are deferred.
4) Super classes and interfaces are all created.
5) After all of the C++ elements are created there is a call to create the Python wrapper for the C++ object.
6) `_jpclass.py` constructs the class by pulling each element with reflection including the methods and fields.  Each of these class construct a python module object PyJP* under `native/python`
7) Customizer are called to add user code to the constructed class.
8) Python class is returned to user

Load pattern is aggressive rather than lazy because it needs to know all types to expose the proper python methods and base class.  Memory is held in global tables for classes and methods and are connected to the jvm instance.  Objects are owned by the PyJP `_jpype` module.

Adding the C++ hooks to call a python routine when a specific attribute is request is likely a 1 hour task if one knows the structure of jpype.  The hook needs to be added to the setResource table and added to the globals.  The PyJPModule type definition then has to add something catch the request for attributes not already in a slot.  Then one mixed cpython module call has to pack the required arguments and pass the return back using the `jp_pythonenv` calls.  Then the python hook must be initialized in the initialized method in `_jclass.py`.  Then if is just a matter of processing through the list of reflected methods to create the return.  If we need more than just `__annotation__`, we just add another hook using the same procedure.  It was a bit harder in the released version as you had communicate with `HostRef` objects.
The only tricky piece that can go wrong is handling the reference counting when talking to cpython.

Hi @matkam, thanks for the report! I believe the intention was to provide both options per the documentation at https://www.vaultproject.io/api/system/wrapping-unwrap.html:

> This endpoint can be used by using a wrapping token as the client token in the API call, in which case the token parameter is not required; or, a different token with permissions to access this endpoint can make the call and pass in the wrapping token in the token parameter. Do not use the wrapping token in both locations; this will cause the wrapping token to be revoked but the value to be unable to be looked up, as it will basically be a double-use of the token!

In other words:

* You can set the wrapped token as the "client" token and make the unwrap call if you are going that route
* Alternatively, if you already have a generic token set on the client and have a *seperate* token you wish to unwrap, that wrapped token can be passed as a parameter to this method.
